critic_raw:
The memory-module anomaly detection paper rests on several implicit claims that the described methodology does not adequately test.

The core mechanistic claim — that the Memory Module reduces CNN representation capacity for anomalous inputs — is never directly verified. The paper infers reduced capacity from downstream PSNR/L2 metrics rather than from direct inspection of memory access patterns (e.g., attention weights to individual memory slots, reconstruction error decomposed by query-memory distance). Without this intermediate evidence, an alternative explanation is equally consistent: the model learns a sharper in-distribution manifold, and the "capacity reduction" framing is post-hoc. Evidence that would resolve this: ablate the memory module entirely and verify that a plain autoencoder encoder-decoder trained only on normal data does NOT achieve comparable anomaly discrimination — which is a non-trivial claim given that reconstruction-based anomaly detection without memory modules is already competitive.

The hybrid anomaly score combining PSNR and L2 between queries and memory items is an unconstrained design choice. PSNR is a nonlinear function of MSE (logarithm), while L2 is linear. Combining them without learned or principled weighting introduces an implicit scale-dependent balance. On near-normal anomalies — precisely the hard cases — PSNR can dominate and saturate, masking the query-memory distance signal. The paper does not report sensitivity of AUC to the weighting of these two components, which would reveal whether the improvement comes from the memory module or from the hybrid scoring.

The Test-Time Updation strategy allows memory items to shift toward normal test-scene statistics during inference. This creates a form of adaptive thresholding that is dataset-specific: the strategy implicitly assumes that test sequences begin with (or contain a sufficient proportion of) normal frames to calibrate the memory. If an anomaly appears early in a test sequence, the updation process may adapt toward anomalous patterns before the anomaly detector fires, suppressing the detection signal. The paper does not report results conditioned on anomaly onset timing within test sequences.

The Separateness Loss and Compactness Loss are described as enhancing diversity and discriminability of memory items, but their interaction with the standard reconstruction objective is not analyzed. In particular, it is possible for the compactness loss to over-compress the normal prototype space to the point where legitimate within-normal variation is treated as anomalous — the 'over-normalization' failure mode. Ablations removing each loss individually would be needed to determine their marginal contributions; the description does not indicate these were performed.

The proxy task framing (reconstruction or future-frame prediction) introduces an additional variable: the paper does not clearly delineate which results correspond to which proxy task, nor is there a systematic comparison between reconstruction-based and prediction-based variants controlling for all other factors. If the prediction variant performs better, that performance could be attributable to the temporal modeling capacity rather than the memory mechanism.
all_issues_raised: The claim that the Memory Module reduces CNN representation capacity for anomalous inputs is inferred from PSNR/L2 outputs rather than directly measured via memory access patterns or attention weights — a plain reconstruction autoencoder without memory may produce similar discrimination.

The hybrid anomaly score combining PSNR (nonlinear, logarithmic) and L2 (linear) lacks principled weighting; on near-normal anomalies PSNR can saturate and dominate, potentially attributing performance gains to the scoring function rather than the memory mechanism.

The Test-Time Updation strategy assumes test sequences contain sufficient normal frames before anomaly onset; if an anomaly appears early, memory items may adapt toward anomalous patterns before detection fires, a failure mode not tested by reporting aggregate AUC.

The Separateness Loss and Compactness Loss interaction with the reconstruction objective is uncharacterized; over-compression of the normal prototype space could flag intra-normal variation as anomalous, yet no per-loss ablation is described.

Reconstruction-based and future-frame prediction proxy tasks are not systematically compared under controlled conditions, conflating the temporal modeling capacity of the prediction variant with the contribution of the memory mechanism.
all_issues_adjudicated: The claim that the Memory Module reduces CNN representation capacity for anomalous inputs is inferred from PSNR/L2 outputs rather than directly measured via memory access patterns or attention weights — a plain reconstruction autoencoder without memory may produce similar discrimination.

The hybrid anomaly score combining PSNR (nonlinear, logarithmic) and L2 (linear) lacks principled weighting; on near-normal anomalies PSNR can saturate and dominate, potentially attributing performance gains to the scoring function rather than the memory mechanism.

The Test-Time Updation strategy assumes test sequences contain sufficient normal frames before anomaly onset; if an anomaly appears early, memory items may adapt toward anomalous patterns before detection fires, a failure mode not tested by reporting aggregate AUC.

The Separateness Loss and Compactness Loss interaction with the reconstruction objective is uncharacterized; over-compression of the normal prototype space could flag intra-normal variation as anomalous, yet no per-loss ablation is described.

Reconstruction-based and future-frame prediction proxy tasks are not systematically compared under controlled conditions, conflating the temporal modeling capacity of the prediction variant with the contribution of the memory mechanism.
